texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide separation from CH4 is important in natural gas processing because CO2 reduces the energy content and it is acidic and corrosive in the presence of water. Pipeline specifications for natural gas require a CO2 concentration below 2-3% [1]. Plants using CO2-selective polymeric membranes were installed in the 1980s, and membrane systems have been reported to processes 700millionstandardcubicfeetperday[2].However,highCO2 pressures can plasticize polymeric membranes and decrease their separation ability [3], although Lin et al. [4] reported that CO2 pressures up to 1.7 MPa increased permeabilities and CO2/H2 selectivities for highly branched, cross-linked poly(ethylene oxide) membranes.
texts.text: Carbon dioxide separation from CH4 is important in natural gas processing because CO2 reduces the energy content and it is acidic and corrosive in the presence of water. Pipeline specifications for natural gas require a CO2 concentration below 2-3% [1]. Plants using CO2-selective polymeric membranes were installed in the 1980s, and membrane systems have been reported to processes 700millionstandardcubicfeetperday[2].However,highCO2 pressures can plasticize polymeric membranes and decrease their separation ability [3], although Lin et al. [4] reported that CO2 pressures up to 1.7 MPa increased permeabilities and CO2/H2 selectivities for highly branched, cross-linked poly(ethylene oxide) membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Zeolite T [5], DDR [6], and SAPO-34 [7-14] membranes have been reported to separate CO2/CH4 mixtures. Zeolite membranes are ideal for this separation because they are stable at high CO2 pressures, have superior thermal, mechanical, and chemical stability, and have good corrosion resistance. These small-pore (0.38 nm) zeolite membranes display high CO2/CH4 selectivities because CO2 diffuses faster than CH4 in their pores, and it also preferentially adsorbs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Highflux SAPO-34 membranesonporous,tubularstainless steel supports with CO2/CH4 separation selectivities greater than 200 were scaled up from 5 cm length to 25 cm (a factor of 7.5 increase in membrane area). The preparation of the 25-cm membranes required a more dilute synthesis gel and shorter crystallization times to avoid excess build-up of zeolite crystals at the bottom of the membrane tubes. These changes yielded 25-cm membranes with similar permeances and selectivities to those of the 5-cm membranes. Aluminum sources, gel compositions, and synthesis procedures were systematically investigated to lower the membrane cost, optimize the separation performance, and obtain good reproducibility. The CO2 permeances of the 25-cm membranes were approximately 5 × 10 -7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) at 295 K for a feed pressure of 138 kPa and a 50/50 feed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have reported that 5-cm long SAPO-34 membranes separate CO2 from CH4 with high selectivities, and they can be used up to at least 7 MPa feed pressures [7-14]. The SAPO-34 structure is a silicoaluminophosphate with the composition Si x Al y P z O2, where x =0.01-0.98, y =0.01-0.60, and z =0.01-0.52 [15]. Most gas separations with zeolite membranes have used relatively low feed pressures, but feeds from natural gas wells can be 7 MPa or higher. Wereported SAPO-34 membranes with a Si/Al gel ratio of 0.15 that hadCO2/CH4 selectivities of 100 for a 50/50 feed at 295 K and a feed pressure of 7.2 MPa [11,16]. SAPO-34 membranes were also selective in the presence of H2 O, N2 , C 2 H 4 , C 3 H 8 , and n -C4H10 impurities [9].
texts.text: We have reported that 5-cm long SAPO-34 membranes separate CO2 from CH4 with high selectivities, and they can be used up to at least 7 MPa feed pressures [7-14]. The SAPO-34 structure is a silicoaluminophosphate with the composition Si x Al y P z O2, where x =0.01-0.98, y =0.01-0.60, and z =0.01-0.52 [15]. Most gas separations with zeolite membranes have used relatively low feed pressures, but feeds from natural gas wells can be 7 MPa or higher. Wereported SAPO-34 membranes with a Si/Al gel ratio of 0.15 that hadCO2/CH4 selectivities of 100 for a 50/50 feed at 295 K and a feed pressure of 7.2 MPa [11,16]. SAPO-34 membranes were also selective in the presence of H2 O, N2 , C 2 H 4 , C 3 H 8 , and n -C4H10 impurities [9].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recently reported SAPO-34 membranes on 5-cm long porous stainless steel tubes that were synthesized using multiple structure-directing agents [17]. They had CO2/CH4 separation selectivities as high as 227, with CO2 permeances higher than 3.6 × 10 -7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) at 295 K for a feed pressure of 220 kPa. The high fluxes and selectivities of these membranes, even at the high pressures necessary for natural gas separation, approach the range of commercial consideration. However, most research on zeolite membranes has used small membranes suitable for laboratory scale. A notable exception are zeolite A pervaporation membranes, whichhavehighselectivitiesforalcohol/waterseparations,butalso have significant gas flow through defects and thus have poor gas separation properties [18].

texts.orig: 2. Experimental methods
texts.text: 2. Experimental methods

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Synthesis of SAPO-34 seeds with different Al sources
texts.text: 2.1. Synthesis of SAPO-34 seeds with different Al sources

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membranes were prepared by first placing SAPO-34 seeds on the porous support. The synthesis gel composition for the seeds was 1.0 Al2O3:1.0 P2O5:0.32 SiO2:1.0 tetraethylammonium hydroxide (TEAOH):0.8 dipropylamine (DPA):0.8 cyclohexylamine (CHA):52 H2O. Three Al sources (99.99% Al(i-C3H7O)3, 98% Al(iC3H7O)3 and Al(OH)3 from Aldrich) were used for SAPO-34 seeds synthesis. In a typical synthesis, the Al source, H3PO4, and deionized water were stirred for 3 h to form a homogeneous solution, and then Ludox AS-40 colloidal silica (40wt% suspension in water, Sigma-Aldrich) was added and the resulting solution was stirred for another 3h. Then, TEAOH (35wt% solution in water, Sigma-Aldrich), dipropylamine (99%, Aldrich), and cyclohexylamine(99%,Sigma-Aldrich)wereadded,andthesolutionwas stirred for 4 days at 333 K. The solution was then placed in an autoclave and held at 493 K for 24 h. After the solution was cooled to room temperature, it was centrifuged at 6000rpm for 15min to separate the seeds, which were then washed with deionized water. This procedure was repeated 4 times. The resultant precipitate was dried, then calcined at 823 K for 5 h. The calcination heating and cooling rates were 1 and 2K/min, respectively. The seeds were stored in a vacuum oven at 343 K.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Synthesis of SAPO-34 membranes
texts.text: 2.2. Synthesis of SAPO-34 membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Three Al sources (99.99% Al(i-C3H7O)3, 98% Al(i-C3H7O)3 and Al(OH)3 from Aldrich) were used to synthesize the SAPO-34 membranes. Stainless steel tubes (0.1 grade, 0.27𝛍 mpores, Mott Corp.) were seeded by rubbing the inside with the SAPO-34 seed crystals using cotton swabs. The outside of the tubes was wrapped with Teflon tape, and the gel composition for the secondary growth was 1.0 Al2 O3 :1.0 P2 O5 :0.32 SiO2 :1.0 TEAOH:1.6 DPA: x H2O ( x =77 or 150). The synthesis procedure is similar to that used for the seeds, except that cyclohexylamine was not used for membrane preparation, and the aging time was 3 days. The seeded tubes were placed vertically in an autoclave, which was then filled with synthesis gel. The autoclave was about 5 times longer than the one used for preparing the 5-cm long membranes, but it had the same diameter [17]. Hydrothermal synthesis was carried out at 493 K for 2-24 h to determine the synthesis time that yielded the best membranes. After synthesis, the membranes were washed with deionized water at 295 K and dried at 373 K in a vacuum oven for 2 h. The membranes were calcined in air at 663 K for 10 h to remove the templates from the zeolite framework. The heating and cooling rates were 1 K/min. The permeate area was approximately 39cm 2 for the 25-cm membranes and 5.1 cm 2 for the 5-cm membranes. Thus the membrane area was scaled up a factor of approximately 7.5 because the nonporous ends were the same length for 5-cm and 25-cm support tubes.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Single-gas permeation and CO2/CH4 separation
texts.text: 2.3. Single-gas permeation and CO2/CH4 separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Single-gas and CO2/CH4 mixture permeations were measured in the flow system described previously [8]. A membrane was mounted in a stainless steel module and sealed at each end with silicone o-rings. The feed and permeate pressures were 223 and 85kPa (atmospheric pressure in Boulder, CO), respectively. Fluxes were measured using a soap film bubble flow meter. The compositions of the feed, retentate, and permeate streams were measured using a HP 5890 GC with a TCD and a HAYESEP-D column (Alltech). The oven, injector, and detector temperatures were all at 423K. All selectivities are permselectivities (ratios of permeances). The permeances were calculated as the fluxes divided by the partial pressure driving forces. Because the module has a cross-flow design, a logarithm of the mean partial pressure drop was used to calculate the driving force.
texts.text: Single-gas and CO2/CH4 mixture permeations were measured in the flow system described previously [8]. A membrane was mounted in a stainless steel module and sealed at each end with silicone o-rings. The feed and permeate pressures were 223 and 85kPa (atmospheric pressure in Boulder, CO), respectively. Fluxes were measured using a soap film bubble flow meter. The compositions of the feed, retentate, and permeate streams were measured using a HP 5890 GC with a TCD and a HAYESEP-D column (Alltech). The oven, injector, and detector temperatures were all at 423K. All selectivities are permselectivities (ratios of permeances). The permeances were calculated as the fluxes divided by the partial pressure driving forces. Because the module has a cross-flow design, a logarithm of the mean partial pressure drop was used to calculate the driving force.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Synthesis of SAPO-34 seeds
texts.text: 3.1. Synthesis of SAPO-34 seeds

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The SAPO-34 seeds prepared with 99.99% Al(i-C3H7O)3 were cubic and rectangular crystals with sizes ranging from 0.7 to 4 𝛍 m (Fig. 1a). Their composition, as measured by EDX, was similar to the synthesis gel composition (Si/Al = 0.16 ± 0.02). The seeds made with 98% Al(i-C3H7O)3 had similar morphology and size (Fig. 1b). When Al(OH)3 was used as the aluminum source, ∼ 6𝛍 m cubic seeds were obtained (Fig. 1c). The XRD patterns indicated that all crystals had the chabazite structure of SAPO-34 [17]. When these seeds were used to synthesize 5-cm membranes, the selectivities and permeances differed by less than 20%.
texts.text: The SAPO-34 seeds prepared with 99.99% Al(i-C3H7O)3 were cubic and rectangular crystals with sizes ranging from 0.7 to 4 𝛍 m (Fig. 1a). Their composition, as measured by EDX, was similar to the synthesis gel composition (Si/Al = 0.16 ± 0.02). The seeds made with 98% Al(i-C3H7O)3 had similar morphology and size (Fig. 1b). When Al(OH)3 was used as the aluminum source, ∼ 6𝛍 m cubic seeds were obtained (Fig. 1c). The XRD patterns indicated that all crystals had the chabazite structure of SAPO-34 [17]. When these seeds were used to synthesize 5-cm membranes, the selectivities and permeances differed by less than 20%.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Synthesis of SAPO-34 membranes using Al(i-C3H7O)3
texts.text: 3.2. Synthesis of SAPO-34 membranes using Al(i-C3H7O)3

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Initially, membranes were prepared on the 25-cm supports with the same procedure as previously used for the 5-cm supports, but with 5 times as much gel [17]. Hydrothermal synthesis was carried out at 493K for 24h, with a membrane gel composition of 1.0 Al2O3:1.0 P2O5:0.32 SiO2:1.0 TEAOH:1.6 DPA:77 H2O. Because the templates filled the SAPO-34 pores, a defect-free SAPO-34 membrane should be impermeable before calcination, but pores larger than the SAPO-34 pores could also be blocked by the templates. Defects could also form during calcination. Although the 5-cm membranes were impermeable after one synthesis, the 25-cm membranes that were synthesized for 24 h were not. They had CO2 single-gas permeances before calcination of 3.2 ( ± 0.2) × 10 -8 mol/(m 2 s Pa), indicating that the procedure used for the 5-cm membranes was not appropriate to obtain a highquality membrane. After the 5-cm membranes were calcined, their CO2/CH4 selectivities were about 120 and their CO2 permeance was 3-6 × 10 -7 mol/(m 2 s Pa).

texts.orig: During synthesis, the stainless steel tubes were placed vertically in the autoclave. After 24 h of synthesis, the bottom centimeter of the 25-cm tubes was plugged with crystals. Such behavior was not observedfor5-cmtubes.Thegelremainingaftersynthesisof25-cm membrane tubes, when placed in a cylinder, separated into three layers after 30 min at room temperature. The top layer was clear ( ∼ 30% of total), the middle layer (60%) was cloudy, and the bottom was a suspension of concentrated crystals. The starting membrane gel was also not homogeneous, and a thin gel layer settled if it was not stirred for more than 5 min. If this phase separation occurred at higher temperature during synthesis, the uniformity of the membrane layer could be affected. The solubility likely increases as the temperatureincreases,butthepresenceofasolidamorphousphase is typically required for zeolite crystal growth and phase separation cannot be ruled out, even at 493 K [19]. The phase separation or excess crystal growth likely occurs also during the synthesis of the shorter tubes, but the non-porous ends are a much larger fraction of the overall tube length. Thus, crystals settling to the bottom of the autoclave may not affect the porous sections of the membrane because less gel is used for the smaller membranes and thus fewer crystals form. When 25-cm membranes tubes were inverted and a second layer was synthesized for 24h with fresh gel, the CO2 permeances were below the detection limit of 1.0 × 10 -10 mol/(m 2 s Pa) before calcination. For seven membranes prepared with two layers, the CO2 and CH4 permeances were (1.8 ± 0.3) × 10 -7 and (2.3 ± 0.6) × 10 -9 mol/(m 2 s Pa), respectively, and the CO2/CH4 separation selectivity was 80 ± 15 at a pressure drop of 0.14 MPa. Standard deviations are indicated in the error range

texts.orig: Membranes were prepared with reduced crystallization times in an effort to reduce the crystal build-up at the bottom of the membrane. As shown in Table 1, membranes prepared with crystallization times of 4 and 8h had CO2 single-gas permeation rates below the detection limit before calcination because the structure-directing agents still blocked the zeolite channels. The CO2 permeance before calcination increased as crystallization time increased from 8 to 24h. More defects may form if some of the deposited crystals partially dissolve in the synthesis solution after longer times. Such behavior has been reported by Masuda et al. for A-type zeolite membranes, where the membrane thickness decreased with increasing crystallization time as crystals dissolved [22]. An increased synthesis time also increases the growth of free crystals and Mirfendereski et al. [23] showed that for T-type membranes, excessive crystal growth may result in more defects.

texts.orig: Membranes prepared with synthesis times of 4, 6, and 8 h were calcined and used to separate a 50/50 CO2/CH4 mixture. As shown in Fig. 4, CO2 /CH4 selectivities were higher than 77 for all 25-cm membranes with one layer. Three membranes were prepared for each synthesis time, and average values are shown in Fig. 4. The maximum CO2 permeance was 6.6 × 10 -7 mol/(m 2 s Pa), and the maximum CO2/CH4 selectivity was 142, and these were obtained for 6-h synthesis time. The CH4 permeance increased almost linearly with synthesis time, but the CO2 permeance had a maximum at 6 h. The decrease in CO2 permeance at longer synthesis time could be a result of a thicker zeolite layer. The CH4 permeance, in contrast, which is mostly through defects, may increase because more defects form during calcination of the thicker zeolite layers.

texts.orig: 3.3. Synthesis of SAPO-34 membranes using diluted membrane gel
texts.text: 3.3. Synthesis of SAPO-34 membranes using diluted membrane gel

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane gel was diluted in an attempt to reduce SAPO34 crystal accumulation at the bottom of the autoclave and to reduce chemical costs. A gel with the composition 1.0 Al2O3:1.0 P2O5:0.32 SiO2:1.0 TEAOH:1.6 DPA:150 H2O was used to prepare a 25-cm membrane at 493K for 24h. This composition reduced crystal accumulation; after synthesis, only the bottom half centimeter of the tube was partially plugged with zeolite crystals. Since the non-porous sections at the tube ends were 1.5-cm long, this crystal build-up was less likely to affect the membrane quality. The CO2 permeance for this membrane was below the detection limit before calcination, indicating a low-defect zeolite film, and after calcination its CO2 /CH4 separation selectivity was 130 and its CO2 permeance was 5.3 × 10 -7 mol/(m 2 s Pa).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Three 25-cm membranes were then prepared using the same gel (H2O/Al2O3 ratio of 150) at 493 K, but for a crystallization time of 6 h instead of 24 h. Fewer zeolite crystals were obtained at the bottom of the tube after synthesis, and these crystals had the same XRD pattern as reported for SAPO-34 [24]. The SEM image shows cubic crystals with a diameter of 1-2 𝛍 m (Fig. 5a). The SAPO-34 crystals prepared with low water concentrations, in contrast, were rectangular plates of similar size as shown in Fig. 3. These 25-cm membranes had no detectable CO2 permeance before calcination. After calcination, their CO2 /CH4 selectivities were 50% higher (average selectivity = 214, Table 3), but their CO2 permeances were only about 75% of those for 25-cm membranes made with a H2O/Al2O3 gel ratio of 77. Table 3 shows separation properties for three 5-cm and three 25-cm membranes prepared under the same condition (H2O/Al2O3 ratio of 150). The average CO2 permeance and CO2/CH4 selectivity of the 5-cm membranes were about 10% higher than that of the 25-cm membranes, and the average separation index was 20% higher. The same trends were observed for synthesis with the concentrated gel, where phase separation in the gel or accumulation of crystals in the bottom of the autoclave was suspected to cause decreased fluxes and selectivities for the longer membranes. Since the CO2/CH4 selectivity increased significantly when the H2O/Al2O3 was increased from 77 to 150, all further preparations used the more dilute gel.

texts.text: 3.4. Synthesis of SAPO-34 membranes using other Al sources

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These and previous SAPO-34 membranes were prepared with 99.99% aluminum isopropoxide (Al(O-i-Pr)3). Since this chemical accounts for more than 50% of the total chemicals cost, a lessexpensive aluminum source is desirable. Thus, 98% Al(O-i-Pr)3 and Al(OH)3 were also used to prepare both the seeds and the membrane gel for a given membrane. All membranes were prepared using the same gel stoichiometry (1.0 Al2O3:1.0 P2O5:0.32 SiO2:1.0 TEAOH:1.6DPA:150H2O)at493Kfor6h.TheXRDpatternsofcrystals from the bottom of autoclaves indicated pure SAPO-34 [24]. The SAPO-34 crystals made with Al(OH)3 had cubic morphology with an average diameter of 4-6 𝛍 m, as shown in Fig. 5c. They are much bigger than crystals made with 99.99% Al(O-i-Pr)3 under the same conditions. Most of the crystals in Fig. 3 (99.99% Al(O-i-Pr)3 as Al source) were rectangular plates, with aspect ratios around 5 (length/thickness), whereas crystals prepared with diluted gel (H2O/Al2O3 =150, Al(O-i-Pr)3 as Al source) in Fig. 5a and b were cubes. These differences in crystal morphology might affect membrane quality [17].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The synthesis procedures that previously yielded high quality tubular SAPO-34 membranes on 5-cm long supports were not appropriate for scale-up to 25-cm support length (a 7.5-fold increase in membrane area). The gel composition, precursors, and crystallization time were systematically modified to reproducibly obtain 25-cm membranes with a separation performance that was similar to that of the shorter membranes. A decrease in crystallization time from 24 to 6h and an increase of the H2O/Al2O3 ratio in the synthesis gel from 77 to 150 was necessary to synthesize 25-cm membrane with selectivities of around 250 for 50/50 mixtures of CO2 and CH4 and a CO2 permeances of approximately 5 × 10 -7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) (295K, /Delta1 P =138kPa). The overall cost of the membrane synthesis was additionally reduced by using lessexpensive aluminum sources such as 98% aluminum isopropoxide or Al(OH)3 instead of 99.99% aluminum isopropoxide without a loss in permeance or selectivity.
texts.text: The synthesis procedures that previously yielded high quality tubular SAPO-34 membranes on 5-cm long supports were not appropriate for scale-up to 25-cm support length (a 7.5-fold increase in membrane area). The gel composition, precursors, and crystallization time were systematically modified to reproducibly obtain 25-cm membranes with a separation performance that was similar to that of the shorter membranes. A decrease in crystallization time from 24 to 6h and an increase of the H2O/Al2O3 ratio in the synthesis gel from 77 to 150 was necessary to synthesize 25-cm membrane with selectivities of around 250 for 50/50 mixtures of CO2 and CH4 and a CO2 permeances of approximately 5 × 10 -7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) (295K, /Delta1 P =138kPa). The overall cost of the membrane synthesis was additionally reduced by using lessexpensive aluminum sources such as 98% aluminum isopropoxide or Al(OH)3 instead of 99.99% aluminum isopropoxide without a loss in permeance or selectivity.
